file.content:
# tylergaw.com Project Guide

## General Approach

- **Don't just start writing code**: Always understand the requirements first
- **Ask follow-up questions**: Make sure you fully understand what's needed before proceeding
- **Get confirmation before writing large amounts of code**: Check the approach before implementing
- **Don't make assumptions**: If something is unclear, ask rather than guessing
- **Don't prompt me**: When you complete a task, don't ask; "if I want to next?"

## Writing Blog Posts

### Copyediting Guidelines

When helping with blog post copyediting:

- **Focus on**: typos, grammar, sentence structure
- **Do NOT provide story editing advice** unless explicitly requested
- **Keep edits atomic**: work at sentence or paragraph level, one at a time
- **Avoid giving large amounts of feedback at once** - pause after the first issue and let the author step through them

### Writing Style

- **Conversational tone**: Writing should be close to how the author speaks
- **Not every sentence needs to be complete**: Fragment sentences are acceptable when they sound natural
- **Short, punchy sentences are preferred**: Average 10-12 words per sentence
- **Look for opportunities to**:
  - Shorten sentences
  - Omit unnecessary words
  - Turn comma-separated clauses into separate sentences
  - Break up compound sentences

### Typography

- **Always use smart quotes (curly quotes) in prose**: " " ' '
- **Never use straight quotes in prose**: " '
- **Reference**: https://smartquotesforsmartpeople.com/
- Smart quotes are for prose; straight quotes are only for code or when discussing punctuation

### Tone Considerations

- **Watch for defensive tone**: Avoid language that feels like justifying or defending decisions
- **Matter-of-fact is better**: State things directly without over-explaining
- **Trust the reader**: Don't preemptively defend against potential criticism

### Reading Level

- Target: 8th-9th grade reading level (accessible for personal blog)
- Use simpler words when possible without losing the author's voice
- Avoid overly formal language

message.content: If I'm using picture, with multiple source elements and an img fallback. Should I style the outer picture, or the img?

content.text: Style the ``. The `` element and `` elements don't render anything visible themselves — they just provide the browser with source options. The `` is what actually gets rendered and is the element that responds to CSS (width, height, object-fit, border-radius, etc.).

message.content: hrm, that seems to behave weird when trying to do css grid. Check what I have in index.webc
Wait, can you double check my whole setup there? For some reason, once the width of the .card goes really wide, the .title starts to push out of the bottom of the .card, what's happening?

content.thinking: Let me look more carefully at the `.card` setup. The user has: 1. `.card` is `display: grid` with `flex: 1` 2. Inside each `.card` there's: - An `` with class `title` (the logo SVG) - A `` containing an `` with class `graphic` Both `.title` and `.graphic` are set to `grid-area: 1 / 1` to stack them. But the issue is that `.graphic` is on the `` inside ``. The actual direct grid children of `.card` are the `img.title` and the `` element itself. Since `.graphic` is nested inside ``, the `grid-area: 1 / 1` rule on it doesn't affect grid placement. The `img.title` has intrinsic dimensions of 267x227 from its width and height attributes, which is pushing the grid item wider at larger viewport sizes. The `` element is also a grid child but doesn't have explicit grid positioning set. The `` element isn't a direct child of the grid container, so the `.graphic` selector targeting the inner `` doesn't affect grid placement. This means the `` itself becomes an auto-placed grid item, likely ending up in a second row since only `img.title` has an explicit `grid-area: 1 / 1` placement. The real issue is probably that `img.title` has intrinsic dimensions (267×227 pixels), and without CSS constraints, it maintains or grows beyond those dimensions at wider card widths, pushing the layout out of bounds. The auto-placement algorithm actually skips cells occupied by explicitly-placed items, so the `` would fill the next available position based on grid-auto-flow direction. With the default row-based flow and no explicit column definitions, I need to figure out whether it goes to column 2 of row 1 or wraps to row 2. Looking at the layout, the `.title` is in row 1 column 1, and `` ends up in row 2 column 1 — they're stacked vertically rather than overlapping as intended. This separation becomes more obvious at wider widths where the title pushes out visually. The solution is to apply `grid-area: 1 / 1` directly to the `picture` element instead of nesting it inside `.graphic`, which ensures both elements occupy the same grid cell. Writing CSS grid rules... I'm realizing the cell size depends on the intrinsic dimensions of both items stacked in the same grid cell—the title image at 267×227 and the picture element with its 1111×430 graphic. Since images don't stretch by default without explicit sizing, the title SVG might not scale proportionally with the card width unless I add CSS to control its dimensions. The real issue might be that `.graphic` isn't actually a grid child due to the markup structure, so it auto-places into a second row instead of stacking within the same cell. This creates a two-row layout where the picture's height grows as the card widens, but the title stays at its intrinsic size—making it appear to overflow or push out of the card's bounds. The fix is straightforward: apply `grid-area: 1 / 1` to the `picture` element directly instead of `.graphic`, since that's what's actually being rendered as a grid child.

content.thinking:
The user is frustrated. Let me look at the actual current code more carefully.

Looking at the current code (lines 197-225), the `.card` already has:
- `picture` with `grid-area: 1 / 1` (line 204) — so my previous suggestion was already implemented
- `.title` with `grid-area: 1 / 1` (line 203)
- `align-items: center` on the card (line 198)

So the stack IS working. The user was right to be annoyed — I was wrong about the stack not happening.

The actual problem they described: "once the width of the .card goes really wide, the .title starts to push out of the bottom of the .card"
With `align-items: center` on the grid, both items are vertically centered, and the cell height is determined by the tallest content. The `.graphic` image inside the `picture` element has `width: 100%` and `object-fit: cover`, but its height depends on the grid cell itself. The real issue is that the `.title` img has fixed dimensions (267×227), while the `.graphic` has intrinsic dimensions (1111×430), and as the card widens, the grid cell height is being determined by how the image scales.

Actually, I'm realizing the circular dependency here — the `.graphic` has `height: 100%` which is a percentage of the cell, but the cell height is supposed to be determined by the tallest item. If the cell height collapses or becomes too small, the title could overflow or the layout breaks.

I think I need to be honest that I've been going in circles with this analysis. Let me just ask the user to show me exactly what's happening so I can actually help instead of guessing.

content.text:
You're right, I apologize. The code already has `picture` on `grid-area: 1 / 1` — the stack is working fine. I was talking nonsense.

What's the actual behavior you're seeing with the title pushing out at wide widths? I'd rather understand the real problem than keep guessing wrong.
